task: I’m looking at a repair estimate that’s a lot higher than I expected, and I don’t want to get boxed into paying for surprises later. What’s the best way to review it and decide what to push back on before I agree to anything?

turns.agent_response:
Use a side-by-side check and only agree to what was actually written down.

Quick review checklist:
1. Pull the original estimate, any revised estimate, and the invoice.
2. Compare them line by line.
3. Mark anything new, vague, or higher priced.
4. Check that the scope matches what was discussed.
5. Verify materials, labor, tax, start date, and any permit responsibility.
6. Look for written approval for any extra work.
7. Confirm license/insurance and warranty terms are included.
8. Hold back on paying disputed items until they’re corrected.

What to push back on:
- Any line item not in the original scope
- “Miscellaneous,” “additional labor,” or other vague charges
- Price increases without written approval
- Materials that weren’t specified
- Extra work that was never signed off on

Simple message you can send:
“I reviewed the estimate and there are items that don’t match what we agreed to. Please send a revised written estimate showing only the agreed work, and provide line-by-line justification for any extra charges before I approve anything.”

If you want, paste the estimate here and I’ll help you flag the problem lines.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right general idea, but it’s too clean. Real problem is the paper games and the excuses.

What I’d want checked first:
- exact scope, in writing
- unit prices on parts, not just a lump sum
- labor hours and rate
- anything marked “as needed,” “allowance,” or “extra”
- disposal, trip charges, permit fees, service fees — all that junk
- whether they’re charging markup on materials
- warranty terms, if any

And if they changed anything from the first number to the second, I’d make them explain every change line by line. No verbal promises. No “we’ll take care of it.” Put it on the estimate.

I’ve had that same kind of mess before — once the pipe job turned into a bill-fight because they wanted to slide in stuff that wasn’t in the estimate. That’s where you stop being polite and start being exact.

If you want it useful, I’d like a tighter checklist for what to strike out and what wording to use when I tell them no.

turns.agent_response:
Use this as your pushback list:

### Strike or question hard
- **Lump sums** with no parts/labor split
- **No unit prices** on parts or materials
- **Labor** with no hours and hourly rate
- **“As needed,” “allowance,” “extra,” “TBD”**
- **Disposal, trip charges, permit fees, service fees** if not spelled out up front
- **Material markup** if they didn’t disclose it
- **Any change from the first estimate** with no line-by-line reason
- **Warranty terms missing or vague**

### What to ask for
- Revised estimate in writing
- Line-by-line breakdown
- Parts quantity and unit price
- Labor hours and rate
- Exact fees and who pays them
- Written explanation for every increase
- Written approval for any added work

### Wording to send
“Please revise the estimate to show the exact scope, parts with unit prices, labor hours and rate, and any fees listed separately. Remove any vague or unapproved charges. If anything changed from the original estimate, list each change line by line with the reason in writing. I’m not approving anything beyond what’s documented.”

### Rule of thumb
If it’s **vague, bundled, or added later**, push back on it.

If you want, I can turn that into a **shorter text-message version** you can send as-is.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little neat, but usable.

I’d tighten the strike list a bit more:
- Any lump sum that hides parts vs labor
- Any line with no unit count
- Any labor with no hours and no rate
- “As needed,” “allowance,” “extra,” “TBD,” “if required” — all of that
- Disposal, trip charge, permit, service fee, shop supply, fuel surcharge — unless it was named up front
- Any material markup they didn’t spell out
- Any change from the first number to the second without a written reason
- Warranty language that’s vague enough to mean nothing

Wording I’d use:

“Revise this estimate in writing. Break out parts, unit prices, labor hours, labor rate, and every fee separately. Remove anything vague or unapproved. List every change from the original estimate line by line with the reason for each change. I’m not approving work beyond what’s written.”

If they start talking instead of writing, I’d stop them there. No verbal fixes. No ‘we’ll square it up later.’ That’s where they start sliding junk in.

If you want, make it even shorter — the kind of line you’d hand them or send by text.

turns.agent_response:
Use this:

**“Please revise the estimate in writing. Break out parts, unit prices, labor hours, labor rate, and every fee separately. Remove anything vague or unapproved. List every change from the original estimate line by line with the reason for each change. I’m not approving work beyond what’s written.”**

If they push back, the line is:

**“No verbal changes. If it’s not in writing, it’s not approved.”**

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’ll do.

Short enough, and it says the right thing: writing only, line by line, no fog.

I’d use the second line too if they start jawing at me:

“No verbal changes. If it’s not in writing, it’s not approved.”

That’s the whole game right there.
